title: The Visible Agent, The Invisible Studio
text:
The Premise
In the 1980s, Max Headroom appeared to be fully computer-generated.
Sharp. Synthetic. Digital.
But behind the illusion: - Human performance
- Manual compositing
- Crafted intelligence
The AI was not real.
The experience was.

title: The Visible Agent, The Invisible Studio
text:
The Inversion
We take that concept and flip it.
Today, the intelligence is real.
But it exists on a spectrum: - From dufus
- To genius
- Often unpredictably in between
AI is powerful—but not yet consistently reliable.

title: The Visible Agent, The Invisible Studio
text:
The Opportunity
What if clients could experience AI at its best…
Without being exposed to its worst?

title: The Visible Agent, The Invisible Studio
text:
The Concept
A cloud-based design studio where:

Clients interact with intelligent AI agents

Agents feel like autonomous creative partners

The experience is seamless, consistent, always on

A single, continuous digital presence.

title: The Visible Agent, The Invisible Studio
text:
The Illusion (By Design)
The agent appears: - Fully capable
- Context-aware
- Creatively reliable
Like a modern digital persona.

title: The Visible Agent, The Invisible Studio
text:
The Reality
Behind every agent:

Human designers

Creative directors

Strategists

Operating quietly in the loop.

title: The Visible Agent, The Invisible Studio
text:
The Model
Human-in-the-loop AI

AI handles speed, iteration, continuity

Humans ensure:

Accuracy

Cultural relevance

Creative quality

Strategic alignment

title: The Visible Agent, The Invisible Studio
text:
Why This Matters
Pure AI: - Fast, but inconsistent
Pure human: - High quality, but slow and expensive
We combine both: - AI interface
- Human assurance

title: The Visible Agent, The Invisible Studio
text:
The Strategy
“Fake it till you make it”—but with intent.
Not deception.
Staged maturity.

Present a stable AI experience today

Use human oversight to:

Correct errors

Train systems

Refine outputs

title: The Visible Agent, The Invisible Studio
text:
The Evolution
Over time:

Less human correction

More autonomous capability

Increasing system intelligence

The illusion becomes reality.

title: The Visible Agent, The Invisible Studio
text:
The Value
Clients get: - Always-on creative partner
- Faster turnaround
- Consistent interaction
- High-quality output
Without AI volatility.

title: The Visible Agent, The Invisible Studio
text:
The Position
AI delivers the experience.
Humans guarantee the outcome.

title: The Visible Agent, The Invisible Studio
text:
The Vision
The Visible Agent.
The Invisible Studio.
A new model for creative work: - Scalable
- Intelligent
- Human-secured

title: AI in Praxis
text:
Welcome & Introduction
Seminar: AI Workflows for Academic Writing
Duration: 90 minutes
Focus: How AI supports — not replaces — scholarship.

Opening context:

Thesis writing challenges: information overload, argument structure, fatigue.
AI can assist with search, synthesis, and writing clarity.
You remain the scholar — AI is your assistant, not your author.

In this session you will:

Learn how to search, synthesise and draft with AI.
Explore ethics and attribution.
Leave with a personal workflow plan.




Greet participants warmly and thank them for joining.
Introduce the title: AI Workflows for Academic Writing.
Explain that the session is compact and hands-on.
Ask everyone to open the live poll or Mentimeter link.
Say: “Tell me your top writing pain point; we’ll use your answers for examples later.”
Mention that poll results will guide the live demos.
Transition: “Let’s look at where AI actually fits into academic work.”
Slide Purpose: Orientation and motivation.
Learning Outcomes addressed: introduction to LO 1–3 orientation.
Facilitator activity: read script.

title: AI in Praxis
text:
What frustrates you most?


  


https://forms.gle/oSL8rGdxmhjioe4x8




Ask everyone to open the live poll or Mentimeter link.
Say: “Tell me your top writing pain point; we’ll use your answers for examples later.”
Mention that poll results will guide the live demos.
Transition: “Let’s look at where AI actually fits into academic work.”
Facilitator activity: launch live poll, note top three issues for later demos.

title: AI in Praxis
text:
Where AI Helps…  and Where It Doesn’t (Yet)

University standards require:

Integrity — no plagiarism or data fabrication.
Originality — ideas must be your own.
Transparency — disclose AI use if required by policy.

AI can assist with:

Rapid literature discovery

Structuring outlines, abstracts, and drafts

Grammar, tone, and citation checks.

Generating sample code or figures from your notes.

AI cannot replace:

Reading and understanding papers.

Critical judgment or design reasoning.

Ethical authorship or data verification.








Tip


Use AI to speed tasks — not to skip thinking.







Where AI Helps — and Where It Doesn’t.
Explain that AI tools act like accelerators, not replacements for academic skills.
Give one concrete example: “If you ask for key papers, always verify titles and DOIs before citing.”
Mention that AI may misread visuals or produce generic text if prompts are vague.
Emphasise that the human remains the researcher; AI does the mechanical side.
Conclude: “We’ll explore how to balance speed and scholarship in today’s session.”
Segment timing: 5–15 min
Learning Outcomes linked: LO #1 (Search & curate), LO #2 (Synthesize literature), LO #3 (Draft with AI) — Knowledge phase
Facilitator task: deliver a 10-minute talk with examples, show a one-page cheat-sheet or diagram of the research lifecycle.

title: AI in Praxis
text:
What’s your research about?

Activity:
In pairs, take turns to describe your current research question.
Prompting Q’s:

What are you studying?

Where are you stuck: finding sources, structuring ideas, or writing?

What would you like AI to help with today?

Timing:

Each pair talk to each other → switch sides after 3 minutes.

Post a 3-sentence summary of your project in the chat.




Announce that it’s time to meet one partner for a quick exchange.

Say: “In your breakout pair, tell each other what you’re researching and what frustrates you most right now.”

Encourage short, focused sharing — one minute setup, one minute response.

After the chat posts appear, read two or three aloud as examples.

Note recurring themes — these will guide which examples you demo later.

Transition line: “Let’s see how we can use AI to find relevant papers for these topics.”
Segment timing: 15–25 min
Learning Outcomes linked: LO #2 (Synthesize literature) — motivation and peer context.
Format: Breakout pairs → plenary chat.
Deliverable: 2-sentence problem statement per participant.

title: AI in Praxis
text:
LO #1: Search & Curate Literature
Find 10 peer-reviewed papers relevant to your topic within 20 minutes.

Activity:

Create the prompt - context is king!

Frame your topic (Boolean+)
Filter for recent peer-reviewed items (2018+)
Check quality on publisher sites (DOI addresses).
Generate a 1-sentence annotation for each paper.
Format result as an ordered list.

Paste your list (10 items + summaries) in chat/shared doc.
Verify one paper: check DOI link to confirm accuracy.




Introduce this as the first hands-on demo of AI in research.

Remind them to treat AI suggestions as leads, not truth.

Explain that we’ll build an annotated bibliography later from these results.

Model a live search using a neutral example topic (e.g., sustainable packaging design).

Walk through each result: check title, author, journal, year.

Ask participants to verify one entry before posting their list.

Summarise: “We’ve just completed the first outcome — a curated, checked mini-bibliography.”

Segment timing: 25 – 40 min Learning Outcome linked: LO #1 (Search & curate). Format: Demo + guided individual practice. Deliverable: Five-paper annotated mini-bibliography (shared text or chat).

title: AI in Praxis
text:
Frame your topic


Formulate Old School Boolean Search
Combine key terms to capture relevant papers and exclude noise using Boolean building blocks








Op.
Function
Example




OR
related terms
“sustainable product” OR “ecodesign”


AND
combine diff. ideas
“ecodesign” AND “manufacturing”


” “
exact phrase
“green design”


*
find word variants
design* → design, designer, designing



 resulting in… 

(“sustainable product” OR “green design”) AND (design* OR develop* OR engineer*)

 Paste into Google Scholar, Scopus, or LLM:
Apply filters: Peer-reviewed, 2018 onwards, English language etc
Frame Your Topic with AI (Boolean+)

Generate a Boolean search string for my topic:
[insert your topic here].

Include:
    •   Synonyms and related terms (use OR)
    •   Two distinct concept groups combined with AND
    •   Quotation marks for multi-word phrases
    •   Wildcards where useful (e.g., design*)

Return the Boolean query on one line ready for Scopus, Google Scholar, or Elicit.




Start by asking: “What are the key ideas in your topic?” (e.g., sustainability + design).

Explain that OR collects synonyms and broadens results; AND combines concept groups.

Show that quotation marks keep multi-word phrases intact.

Demonstrate the asterisk for word variations – “design*” captures design, designer, designing.

Run the example live in a database or AI tool.

Emphasise that Boolean logic helps you control results – it’s not random guessing.

Transition: “Now that we’ve built a good search string, we can feed it into AI tools to generate candidate papers.”

title: AI in Praxis
text:
Creating Prompts
Build and refine LLM prompts for literature discovery.

What is a prompt?
A short instruction or question you give to an AI model.
It guides the model’s task, scope, and format of response.

Find 10 peer-reviewed papers from 2018–2025  
on sustainable or green product design, development, or engineering.  
Include title, author, year, DOI, and one-line summary.  
Mark any uncertain citation with [VERIFY].

Prompt structure

Role – tell the AI who it should act as.

Task – describe exactly what to do.

Context / criteria – add limits or filters.

Output format – specify how results should appear.

Optimise Your Prompt with the LLM
You can also ask the LLM to co-create or optimise the prompt with you.

You are an academic search assistant helping me locate recent, peer-reviewed papers.
Improve my prompt for clarity and reproducibility.

Keep all key terms and add filters for:
    •   peer-reviewed journals
    •   date range (2018–present)
    •   output format (table)
Return your optimised version.

title: AI in Praxis
text:
Build and refine LLM prompts for literature discovery.

Detailed Prompt Sample

You are a research assistant helping me locate recent, peer-reviewed papers.
Topic focus: [YOUR TOPIC]

Please do the following:
1. Return a list of **10 peer-reviewed journal articles from 2018 onwards**.  
2. For each article, include:
   - Title  
   - Author(s)  
   - Year  
   - DOI or publisher link  
   - A **one-sentence summary** of the study’s aim or contribution.  
3. Exclude:
   - Preprints, blogs, or non-peer-reviewed sources  
   - Duplicates or incomplete references  
4. If uncertain about any citation, mark it with `[VERIFY]` instead of guessing a DOI.  
5. Present the output as a simple table.

Example output format:
| # | Title | Author(s) | Year | DOI | Summary |  
|---|--------|------------|------|------|----------|  
| 1 | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... |

Respond only with verifiable results.

title: AI in Praxis
text:
LO #2: Synthesize the Literature & find the Gap

Short Prompt:
Write a 150–200 word paragraph summarising what’s known + the gap.
Sample Prompt:

Using these 10 papers, write a 150-word paragraph that\
(a) summarises the main approaches,
(b) identifies what remains unresolved,
(c) ends with the gap my project will address.

Your task (10 min):

Create your own prompt.

Draft your paragraph.

Paste it in the shared document/chat.

Label your post: “LIT-GAP PARA – [Your Name].”

Checklist:

Clarity
Evidence from sources
Explicit gap statement




Announce that it’s time to turn search results into synthesis.

Encourage writing first in plain language, then refining with AI if they wish.

Remind them to cite or note authors within the paragraph.

Say: “Keep your paragraph factual and show where the gap is—don’t just list papers.”

After 10 minutes, invite two volunteers to read theirs aloud.

Transition: “We’ll now swap for quick peer feedback to see how these paragraphs read to others.”

Segment timing: 40 – 50 min Learning Outcome linked: LO #2 (Synthesize literature) Format: Individual writing → shared submission Deliverable: 150–200 word literature-gap paragraph per participant

title: AI in Praxis
text:
Rapid Peer Feedback
(Small-Group Critique)

Task:
Strengthen clarity and logic in your literature-gap paragraph.
Group setup:
3 participants per group.
Task (10 min total):

Exchange your paragraphs.
Give 4 comments each:

Is the prompt well-structured?

Is the paragraph clear and concise?

Is the evidence properly linked to sources?

Is the gap explicit and meaningful?


Quick rubric (1–5 scale):
1 = unclear 3 = understandable 5 = compelling
Deliverable:
3 short comments + rating per peer.



Direct students to breakout groups of three.

Say: “Each person reads another’s paragraph and gives three quick, specific comments.”

Emphasise constructive tone—focus on clarity and connection to literature.

Display the 1–5 scale so everyone uses a common measure.

Keep time: 3 minutes each round, one minute to wrap up.

After 10 minutes, call everyone back and ask for one insight from the activity.

Transition: “Now that we’ve practised synthesis, let’s try generating and refining an abstract using AI.”

Segment timing: 50 – 60 min Learning Outcome linked: LO #2 (Synthesize literature) — peer feedback and revision. Format: Small groups of three. Deliverable: Peer comments and numeric rating for each paragraph.

title: AI in Praxis
text:
LO #3: From Draft to Abstract

Create an Abstract with AI:
Produce a ≤ 250-word abstract using an AI-assisted prompt, then edit for clarity and accuracy.
Demo steps (facilitator):

Paste a 1-page methods + results section into the LLM.

Run the “Structured Abstract” prompt.



Create a structured abstract (≤ 250 words) from this text.
Keep numbers and claims as given, and
mark any you’re unsure of with [CHECK].


Review the output for structure (IMRaD form – Intro, Methods, Results, Discussion).

Run a second prompt to humanise tone and insert [CHECK] tags where facts need verification.
Paste your before/after pair in the shared document.

Edit Checklist:

Accuracy
Clarity
Logical flow
Word limit ≤ 250




Introduce this as our “AI draft and refine” moment.

Demonstrate live with a neutral sample text (e.g., study on design thinking methods).

Show how to run the first prompt and what a structured abstract looks like.

Emphasise human editing afterwards — highlight where AI misses nuance or adds generic phrasing.

Ask participants to try the prompt with their own data if they have it, or use the sample provided.

Discuss how the [CHECK] tags help track verification needs.

Transition: “Next, we’ll look at how to handle ethics and AI use in real publication scenarios.”

Segment timing: 60 – 70 min Learning Outcome linked: LO #3 (Draft with AI). Format: Live demo + short individual practice. Deliverable: Before/after abstract pair + reflection checklist.

title: AI in Praxis
text:
Ethics in Practice — Hallucination & Attribution Challenge

Goal (LO #7 – Ethics & Attribution):
Identify ethical responses when AI invents or misattributes sources.
Scenario:
An AI tool suggests a “seminal” paper that doesn’t exist.
Your co-author wants to include it to strengthen your argument.
Group Task (10 min):

Discuss → What 3 steps would you take to verify and respond?

Draft → One sentence your lab could add to its SOP to prevent this.

Post → Your SOP sentence + 3 steps in the shared doc.

Reference Checklist:

Search the DOI or publisher site
Cross-check citations
Disclose AI use honestly




Read the scenario aloud to set the tone.

Emphasise that “hallucination” means a fabricated reference or data point.

Instruct small groups (3–4 people) to identify a verification sequence—what would they do first?

Suggest steps like: confirm DOI, find real sources, note corrections transparently.

After 8 minutes, call them back for 2-minute sharebacks.

Summarise common themes: verification, documentation, disclosure.

Transition: “Let’s hear a few of your lab policy statements before we wrap up.”

Segment timing: 70 – 82 min Learning Outcome linked: LO #7 (Ethics & Attribution) Format: Small-group scenario discussion + plenary shareback Deliverable: 1-sentence lab SOP statement + 3 verification steps posted to shared doc

title: AI in Praxis
text:
Sharebacks

Consolidate insights.

Read your 1-sentence SOP statement. (voluntary)

What are good practices? For instance:

Always verify sources manually.

Keep an audit trail of AI prompts and outputs.

Use transparent disclosure language.





Invite 2–3 groups to share their SOP statements aloud.

Affirm consistent points (verification, disclosure, authorship).

Encourage quick clarifying questions from the group.

Keep responses concise; flag deeper issues for follow-up resources.

Transition line: “We’ll finish by capturing the key take-aways and linking them back to the outcomes we began with.”
“The purpose of this exercise is not to avoid AI, but to use it with integrity and precision.”

Segment timing: 40 min Learning Outcomes linked: Reinforces LO #7 (Ethics & Attribution) and transitions to LO #8 (Workflow Design). Format: Plenary shareback + facilitated discussion Deliverable: Shared SOP statements and verbal Q&A

title: AI in Praxis
text:
Q + A


“What’s one question or concern you still have about using AI in your writing?”




Encourage quick clarifying questions from the group.

Keep responses concise; flag deeper issues for follow-up resources.

Transition line: “We’ll finish by capturing the key take-aways and linking them back to the outcomes we began with.”

Segment timing: 40 min Learning Outcomes linked: Reinforces LO #7 (Ethics & Attribution) and transitions to LO #8 (Workflow Design). Format: Plenary shareback + facilitated discussion Deliverable: Shared SOP statements and verbal Q&A

title: AI in Praxis
text:
LO #8: Wrap-Up & Reflection

Goal ( – Workflow Design):
Sketch your own AI-augmented academic workflow for your next project (submit for next meeting).
Task (5 min):
Create a quick workflow table with these elements:

Phase: Which part will you augment first? (e.g. literature review)
Tool choice: Which AI tool(s) will you test? (e.g. Elicit + Scite)
Checkpoint: How will you verify outputs? (e.g. Verify DOIs weekly)
Timeline: When will you run this and who will review it? (e.g. 2 weeks)

Closing reflection:

“Name one change you’ll make in how you use AI for your research this month.”




Explain that this final activity ties all outcomes together through a personal action plan.

Encourage students to be realistic—choose one manageable workflow phase to pilot.

Remind them to include verification and disclosure steps in their plan.

Ask for one volunteer to share their workflow idea aloud.

End with: “AI is here to support disciplined creativity and efficient scholarship—use it well, use it ethically.”

Thank everyone for participating and share the post-session survey link.

Segment timing: 88 – 90 min Learning Outcome linked: LO #8 (Workflow Design) Format: Individual reflection → optional short share Deliverable: Personal workflow plan (mini table)

title: Spotted Ape
text:

The Differentiator

The Differentiator

title: Spotted Ape
section: Ready to Close the Execution Gap?
text:

→ thespottedape.com → marcelmare.com

THESPOTTEDAPE
    
    
      
    

    
      
        Idea
        Work
        Case
        About
        Book
      
    
  











The Deaf Leading the Blind: What Silicon Valley’s LLM’s Cannot See

“…They have mouths, but cannot speak, eyes, but cannot see. They have ears, but cannot hear, noses, but cannot smell… Those who make them will be like them, and so will all who trust in them.” (Psalm 115:4–8, NIV)


I recently found myself rewatching See No Evil, Hear No Evil. It is a minor 1989 comedy that normally would not get a second viewing. The hilarious Richard Pryor plays Wally, a blind man who refuses to acknowledge his blindness (Figure 1), and Gene Wilder plays Dave, a deaf man who has become remarkably good at pretending to hear. Together they blunder through a murder mystery, each borrowing the faculty the other lacks, each performing a competence neither possesses. Wally strides into bar brawls while Dave shouts directions; Dave borrows Wally’s ears to detect approaching sirens. The comedy turns, precisely, on the gap between performed and actual perception (Figure 2). On reflection, it turns out to be a surprisingly accurate description of artificial intelligence, specifically visual-focussed Large Language Models (LLM).







Figure 1: In this subway sequence, Wally (Pryor) performs “simulated sight”, using memorized cues to mask his blindness.








Figure 2: During the job interview, Wally and Dave attempt to communicate across a complete sensory disconnect.




The joke, as these things go, has aged rather well. Silicon Valley has spent the better part of a decade equipping AI with eyes. Visual encoders. Camera inputs. The image-upload button presented to us with confidence that the machine can now see. What the product announcements rarely mention is the architecture sitting behind the lens. The model processes visual information by first converting it into words; then reasoning about those words; and, finally describing what it believes to be there. All with the practiced confidence of a man who cannot see the bar fight he has just walked into.


The Wally Problem
Silicon Valley’s flagship general-purpose models still have the language bottleneck firmly in place. The chatbot works, ostensibly, because the partnership is all it has. This is the Wally problem. Vision for most multimodal AI systems relies on a process of narration, not perception. Pixels enter; the language model converts them into tokens; the tokens are then reasoned about (Su et al., 2025). Under light cognitive load, this works well enough. Under heavier demands requiring genuine inference, the visual attention collapses, and the model does what researchers have delicately termed “relying on linguistic priors” (Qian et al., 2026). It does a Wally. It narrates the world it expects rather than the one in front of it. In other words, it does not merely struggle to see. It actively replaces sight with pre-trained confidence.

Qian, Z., Ma, Y., Ouyang, Z., Wang, Z., Xu, Z., Luo, F., Liu, X., Ge, Z., Guo, Y., & Han, J. (2026). Cognitive pivot points and visual anchoring: Unveiling and rectifying hallucinations in multimodal reasoning models. arXiv Preprint arXiv:2604.10219. https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.10219
An instinctive counter-argument could point to Tesla. If AI cannot see, how precisely is a two-tonne vehicle able to negotiate an American intersection without incident? It is a fair question. The answer, however, is not that Tesla has solved the vision problem. Tesla’s v12 architecture maps pixels directly to steering and braking outputs, bypassing language altogether. There is no Dave whispering directions to Wally here. The blind man has simply been removed from the vehicle. The car does not name a stop sign before responding to it; it encodes the sign as a spatial object with associated coordinates and probabilities, and acts accordingly.
Another apparent aesthetic counter-argument are the visual generation tools of which Midjourney is the prime example. The remarkable images these generative models produce seem to have mastered the disconnect. The light behaves. The geometry holds. The shadows fall where shadows ought to fall. One is almost persuaded. One is reminded of Wally discovering, late in the film, that he has become a rather convincing actor. Which is not the same thing as having recovered his sight.
What Midjourney has mastered is the aesthetics of plausibility (Su et al., 2025). Through heroic quantities of human feedback, it has learned the statistical grammar of what beautiful images look like—the precise pixel distribution that human evaluators label as “a convincing glass of wine,” “a plausible city at night,” “dramatic without being ridiculous.” It does not know that glass shatters, that wine is liquid, or that the laws of refraction are not merely decorative suggestions. It is navigating a latent manifold of human visual taste with extraordinary precision.
The results are often beautiful. They are also, in any domain where spatial truth is the point, almost completely beside it.
That domain is science. When researchers recently evaluated leading multimodal models on scientific tasks requiring perception, interpretation, and reasoning over real data, performance was strikingly limited. In the Scientists’ First Exam benchmark, state-of-the-art systems achieved only around 30% accuracy, revealing substantial gaps in their ability to integrate visual information with scientific reasoning (Zhou et al., 2025).

Zhou, Y., Wang, Y., He, X., Xiao, R., Li, Z., Feng, Q., Guo, Z., Yang, Y., Wu, H., Huang, W., Wei, J., Si, D., Yao, X., Bu, J., Huang, H., Fu, T., Tang, S., Fei, B., Zhou, D., … Bai, L. (2025). Scientists’ first exam: Probing cognitive abilities of MLLM via perception, understanding, and reasoning. arXiv Preprint arXiv:2506.10521. https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.10521
The vocabulary was intact. The perception was absent. Wally, as it turns out, knows all the right words for a bar fight.
This failure is, in one register, an academic finding. In another, it is alarming. Engineers developing collaborative tools for blind and low-vision users have discovered that AI-mediated visual description collapses on the very information those users most need: spatial structure (Li et al., 2026). Where is the annotation relative to the diagram? Which cluster corresponds to which label? The visual grammar of collaborative work—arrangement, proximity, grouping as semantic signal—evaporates in translation. A system that cannot genuinely parse space cannot reconstruct it for the people whose relationship to space is already mediated and precarious. Asking Wally to give directions, in other words, has consequences that extend well beyond comedy.

Li, M., Yang, H., Sanda, N., & Das, M. (2026). Idea11y: Enhancing accessibility in collaborative ideation for blind or low vision screen reader users. Proceedings of the 2026 CHI Conference on Human Factors in Computing Systems (CHI ’26). https://doi.org/10.1145/3772318.3790878

Su, Z., Xia, P., Guo, H., Liu, Z., Ma, Y., Qu, X., Liu, J., Li, Y., Zeng, K., Yang, Z., Li, L., Cheng, Y., Ji, H., He, J., & Fung, Y. R. (2025). Thinking with images for multimodal reasoning: Foundations, methods, and future frontiers. arXiv Preprint arXiv:2506.23918. https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.23918
The field is not entirely unaware of the predicament. A growing body of research advocates for what its proponents call “thinking with images” rather than thinking about them. In other words, reasoning natively in visual latent space, rather than routing every observation through the linguistic bottleneck first (Su et al., 2025). Whether this amounts to a genuine architectural reckoning or a more sophisticated performance of sight is, for now, an open question.
The partnership, for the moment, endures. Wally strides forward; Dave shouts what he thinks he lip-read. The demos are polished. The press releases are stirring. The benchmarks, carefully selected, are excellent.
The bar fight, however, is still going on. And it is becoming increasingly difficult, from the outside, to tell which punches are actually landing—and which are connecting, with great conviction, with the empty air.
The bar fight is still going on. Dave is still shouting directions. Wally is still swinging and occasionally, by some combination of statistical fortune and well-trained instinct, connecting. Whether that constitutes fighting, or merely a well-coordinated performance of multiple agents, is the question that remains open.

title: The Deaf Leading the Blind
text:
The Claim
Silicon Valley has spent the better part of a decade equipping AI with eyes.

Visual encoders

Camera inputs

The image-upload button

Presented with quiet confidence.
The machine can now see.

So I want to talk about something that’s been sitting with me for a while now.
There’s a big claim floating around in the tech world. That AI can see. Not just process images — but actually see.
Silicon Valley has spent the better part of a decade equipping AI with eyes. Visual encoders. Camera inputs. The image-upload button, presented with the quiet confidence that the machine can now see.
And I think it’s worth slowing down on that one. Because I recently re-watched an old film. And honestly, it explained the whole thing better than any research paper I’ve read.

title: The Deaf Leading the Blind
text:
The Film
See No Evil, Hear No Evil — 1989

Richard Pryor plays Wally — completely blind

Gene Wilder plays Dave — completely deaf

Together, they solve a murder.
From the outside: one capable person.
From the inside: two incomplete systems.

The film is called See No Evil, Hear No Evil. 1989. Richard Pryor and Gene Wilder. It’s a comedy — a murder mystery — and the premise is simple.
Richard Pryor plays Wally — completely blind. Gene Wilder plays Dave — completely deaf. And somehow, together, they have to solve a crime.
From the outside, they look like one capable person. But from the inside — two incomplete systems pretending to be whole.

title: The Deaf Leading the Blind
text:
The Performance
Wally strides into the bar brawl.
Dave shouts directions.

“Left!”

“Duck!”

“Watch out!”

Neither has the faculty the other is lending them.
They are performing competence.

Here’s what makes it funny — and also, weirdly, quite insightful.
Wally strides confidently into a bar brawl. Dave is shouting directions — “Left! Duck! Watch out!” Wally borrows Dave’s eyes. Dave borrows Wally’s ears.
And somehow they muddle through. But neither of them actually has the faculty the other is lending them. They’re performing competence.

title: The Deaf Leading the Blind
text:
The Gap
The comedy turns on exactly one thing:
The distance between what they appear able to do…
and what they can actually perceive.
That gap is where modern AI lives.

The comedy turns entirely on that gap — between what they appear able to do, and what they can actually perceive.
That gap? That’s where modern AI lives.

title: The Deaf Leading the Blind
text:
The Architecture
When you upload a photo to an AI chatbot:
Image → Visual Encoder → Tokens → Language Model → Response
The language model — the “brain” —
cannot understand images.
It is fundamentally, stubbornly textual.

When you upload a photo to an AI chatbot, there are essentially two parts working together. A visual system that processes the image — and a language model that does the talking.
And here’s the thing. The language model — the brain part — cannot actually understand images. It’s fundamentally and stubbornly a text system.

title: The Deaf Leading the Blind
text:
The Translation
So what actually happens?
The image is converted into words first.
Then the language model reads those words.
Then it responds.

So what actually happens? The image gets converted into words first. Into tokens. Then the language model reads those tokens and responds.
So it’s not seeing your photo. It’s reading a description of your photo. And then talking confidently about that description.

title: The Deaf Leading the Blind
text:
The Reality
It is not seeing your photo.
It is reading a description of your photo.
Then talking confidently from that description.
Vision, for most AI systems, is not perception.
It is narration.

That’s the Wally problem. Vision, for most of these AI systems, is not perception. It’s narration.
When things are simple — good lighting, familiar objects — it works well enough. Because the translation from image to words is easy. The system has seen millions of similar examples.
But the moment things get complicated — unusual angles, cluttered scenes, anything that actually requires reasoning about space — the wheels come off.

title: The Deaf Leading the Blind
text:
When It Works
Simple scenes. Clear lighting. Familiar objects.
The system does well.
Because the translation into words is straightforward.
It has seen millions of similar examples.

When the image is simple — clear lighting, common objects — the system does well. Because it’s seen millions of similar examples. So the translation into words is easy, and the response sounds right.

title: The Deaf Leading the Blind
text:
When It Doesn’t
Novel configurations. Spatial ambiguity. Cluttered scenes.
The visual attention collapses.
The model does what researchers politely call:
“relying on linguistic priors.”

But the moment things get messy — low light, strange angles, unusual situations — that’s when things fall apart.
The visual attention collapses, and the model does what researchers have politely termed “relying on linguistic priors.” Which is a careful way of saying: it stops looking and starts guessing.

title: The Deaf Leading the Blind
text:
The Confabulation
When it isn’t sure, it doesn’t say “I don’t know.”
It guesses.
Confidently.
It knows what usually appears in a kitchen —
so it tells you there’s a microwave on the counter.
Even if there isn’t one.

And here’s the part that should give you pause. When the AI isn’t sure, it doesn’t say “I don’t know.” It guesses. Confidently.
Because it’s trained on patterns of language. It knows what usually appears in a kitchen. So if it sees something vaguely kitchen-like, it might tell you there’s a microwave on the counter. Even if there isn’t one.
It’s not seeing reality. It’s predicting what should be there.

title: The Deaf Leading the Blind
text:
The Wally Problem
It is not seeing reality.
It is predicting what should be there.
Wally does not merely struggle to see.
He replaces sight with confidence.

Researchers have a polite name for this. They call it relying on linguistic priors. I’d call it: Wally walking confidently into a bar fight he cannot see.
Wally doesn’t merely struggle to see. He actively replaces sight with confidence. And that is exactly what these systems do.

title: The Deaf Leading the Blind
text:
“But What About Tesla?”
If AI can’t see — how is a two-tonne car
navigating a busy intersection on its own?
Fair question.

Now — someone’s always going to raise this. If AI can’t see, how is a two-tonne car navigating a busy intersection on its own?
That’s a fair question. And the answer is actually really interesting.

title: The Deaf Leading the Blind
text:
The Workaround
Tesla’s system skips the language step entirely.
Camera → Direct Action (steering, braking, speed)
No words. No narration. Just maths and movement.
In other words:
Dave quietly removed Wally from the vehicle.

Tesla’s system doesn’t work like a chatbot. It doesn’t convert what the camera sees into words first. It goes straight from pixels to action — steering, braking, speed. No narration. No language step. Just maths and movement.
Which means, in our film analogy — Dave didn’t fix the problem. He just quietly removed Wally from the vehicle. The blind man isn’t there anymore. So there’s nobody to mislead.

title: The Deaf Leading the Blind
text:
The Lesson
That is a workaround — not a solution.
General-purpose AI — the chatbots, the assistants —
still have the language bottleneck firmly in place.
The partnership is all they have.

That’s a workaround. Not a solution.
And Silicon Valley’s general-purpose models — the chatbots, the assistants — still have that language bottleneck firmly in place. The partnership between the visual system and the language model is all they have.

title: The Deaf Leading the Blind
text:
The Beautiful Images
What about tools like Midjourney?
The light behaves. The shadows fall correctly.
The geometry holds.
Surely that is understanding vision?

Right, but what about tools like Midjourney? You’ve seen the images. Perfect light. Convincing shadows. Physics that looks right.
Surely that means AI understands vision?

title: The Deaf Leading the Blind
text:
The Aesthetics of Plausibility
Not quite.
What it has mastered is the statistical grammar
of what humans find convincing.
It does not know that glass shatters.
It does not know that wine is liquid.
It has learned where shadows tend to fall
in images that humans approved of.

Not quite. What it’s mastered is what you could call the aesthetics of plausibility. Through enormous amounts of human feedback, it’s learned the statistical grammar of what humans find beautiful. The precise combination of pixels that we label “convincing.”
But it doesn’t know that glass shatters. It doesn’t know that wine is liquid. It doesn’t understand refraction — it’s just learned where shadows tend to fall in images that humans approved of.

title: The Deaf Leading the Blind
text:
The Actor
It is a bit like Wally, later in the film,
discovering he has become a rather convincing actor.
Which is not the same thing
as having recovered his sight.

It’s a bit like Wally, later in the film, discovering he’s become a rather convincing actor. Which is not the same thing as having recovered his sight.
The results are often genuinely beautiful. But beautiful is not the same as accurate. And in any situation where spatial truth actually matters, those two things are very different.

title: The Deaf Leading the Blind
text:
The Benchmark
Researchers recently tested leading AI systems
on scientific tasks requiring spatial reasoning.
State-of-the-art systems scored:

title: The Deaf Leading the Blind
text:
Wally Knows the Words
Wally knows all the right words for a bar fight.
He just can’t see where the punches are coming from.

Wally, it turns out, knows all the right words for a bar fight. He just can’t see where the punches are coming from.

title: The Deaf Leading the Blind
text:
The Accessibility Problem
Engineers building tools for blind and low-vision users
have found that AI visual description
collapses on exactly what those users need most:
Spatial structure.

Where is this annotation relative to that diagram?

Which cluster belongs to which label?


And then there’s the accessibility problem. This is where it becomes more than an academic finding.
Engineers building tools for blind and low-vision users have found that AI-assisted visual description falls apart on exactly the information those users need most. Spatial structure. Where is this annotation relative to that diagram? Which cluster belongs to which label?

title: The Deaf Leading the Blind
text:
When Narration Isn’t Enough
The visual grammar of how things are arranged —
proximity, grouping, position —
that is semantic information.
It evaporates in translation.
Asking Wally to give directions
has consequences beyond a punchline.

The visual grammar of how things are arranged — proximity, grouping, position — that’s semantic information. And it evaporates in translation.
A system that cannot genuinely parse space cannot reconstruct it for the people whose relationship to space is already mediated and precarious.
Asking Wally to give directions has consequences that go well beyond a punchline.

title: The Deaf Leading the Blind
text:
What’s Coming
A growing push to build AI that reasons visually —
natively, in image space —
rather than routing everything through language first.
Thinking with images, not about them.
Whether that is a genuine fix,
or a more sophisticated performance of sight —
still an open question.

To be fair, the field isn’t completely ignoring this. There’s a growing push to build AI that reasons visually — natively, in image space — rather than routing everything through language first. Thinking with images, rather than about them.
Whether that turns out to be a genuine fix, or just a more sophisticated performance of sight, is genuinely still an open question.

title: The Deaf Leading the Blind
text:
The Reality Check
Next time you see an AI demo that looks like magic:
It is not seeing the world.
It is receiving a description of the world —
and talking confidently from that.

So next time you see an AI demo that looks like magic — just remember what’s actually happening underneath.
In most cases, it’s not seeing the world. It’s receiving a description of the world, and talking confidently from that.

title: The Deaf Leading the Blind
text:
The Situation
Dave is still shouting directions.
Wally is still striding forward like he owns the place.
The demos are polished.
The press releases are stirring.
The benchmarks, carefully selected, are excellent.
The bar fight is still going on.

It’s Dave shouting directions. And Wally striding forward like he owns the place.
Which — honestly — sometimes works out fine. Until it doesn’t.
The demos are polished. The press releases are stirring. The benchmarks, carefully selected, are excellent. But the bar fight is still going on.

title: The Deaf Leading the Blind
text:
The Question
From the outside, it is becoming increasingly difficult
to tell which punches are actually landing —
and which are connecting, with great conviction,
with the empty air.

And it is becoming increasingly difficult, from the outside, to tell which punches are actually landing — and which are connecting, with great conviction, with the empty air.
Let me know in the comments — do you trust AI to actually see, or not? Because the more you understand what’s happening under the hood, the better placed you are to use these tools well.
And if this kind of breakdown is useful to you, hit subscribe — because the tech is moving fast, and it’s worth knowing what’s real.

title: The Deaf Leading the Blind
text:
The Deaf Leading the Blind
What Silicon Valley’s AI Cannot See
Marcel Maré

Thanks for watching.
